title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Childhood Triangulation
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy helps survivors of childhood triangulation heal body-based trauma and regulate their nervous system.
body:
Understanding Childhood Triangulation and Its Lasting Impact

Childhood triangulation is a covert form of emotional manipulation where a child is pulled into the conflicts or dysfunctional dynamics between caregivers. Instead of direct communication between parents or caregivers, the child becomes the messenger, scapegoat, or buffer. This dynamic creates chronic stress, confusion, and a deep sense of instability in the child's nervous system. As Bessel van der Kolk notes in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma isn't just a psychological wound—it embeds itself in the body, shaping how we react, relate, and regulate our emotions even decades later.

Survivors of childhood triangulation often find themselves stuck in patterns of hypervigilance, anxiety, and difficulty trusting their own perceptions. Traditional talk therapy may provide some relief, but the body often holds onto these traumatic imprints in ways that words alone cannot reach. This is where somatic therapy becomes a critical path toward healing.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is an approach that integrates the mind and body to address trauma and emotional dysregulation. It is grounded in the understanding that trauma is stored in the body’s nervous system and that healing must involve bodily awareness and regulation. Peter Levine, a pioneer in trauma therapy, developed Somatic Experiencing to help clients track physical sensations, discharge trapped energy, and restore nervous system balance.

Stephen Porges' Polyvagal Theory provides a scientific framework for understanding how the autonomic nervous system responds to threat and safety cues. Somatic therapy helps survivors of childhood triangulation renegotiate these nervous system responses to chronic relational stress.

How Childhood Triangulation Affects the Nervous System

When a child is triangulated, the nervous system is caught in a persistent state of alert or freeze, unable to find safety or predictability. This chronic activation of the sympathetic (fight/flight) or dorsal vagal (freeze/shutdown) branches can result in dysregulation that persists into adulthood.

Deb Dana, a leading clinician in Polyvagal-informed therapy, emphasizes that without a felt sense of safety, the nervous system remains trapped in defensive states, making it difficult to engage in healthy relationships or self-regulation.

Survivors might experience:

  • Heightened anxiety or panic attacks
  • Emotional numbing or dissociation
  • Difficulty setting boundaries or trusting others
  • Chronic feelings of guilt, shame, or responsibility for others’ emotions

Why Somatic Therapy Makes a Difference

Unlike traditional talk therapy that focuses primarily on cognition and verbal processing, somatic therapy works directly with the body’s sensations and nervous system responses. It helps survivors recognize the physical manifestations of triangulation trauma and develop tools to regulate their nervous system in real-time.

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, highlights that trauma disrupts the integration of body and mind. Somatic therapy restores this connection by teaching clients to sense their internal states and respond more adaptively.

“Healing trauma requires more than understanding it intellectually — it demands that we reconnect with our bodies and learn to regulate the nervous system from the inside out.” — Bessel van der Kolk

Core Somatic Techniques for Survivors of Triangulation

Several somatic modalities and techniques are particularly effective for addressing childhood triangulation trauma:

  • Body Awareness and Tracking: Learning to notice subtle bodily sensations related to stress or safety helps survivors interrupt automatic reactions.
  • Breath Regulation: Controlled breathing exercises can activate the parasympathetic nervous system, helping to downregulate hyperarousal.
  • Movement and Gesture: Small movements or grounding gestures can release trapped fight/flight energy and signal safety to the brain.
  • Resourcing: Identifying internal or external sources of safety and strength helps build resilience.
  • Pendulation: Developed by Peter Levine, this technique involves moving between states of distress and calm to gradually expand tolerance for difficult sensations.

Integrating Somatic Therapy with Internal Family Systems (IFS)

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model pairs well with somatic approaches. Childhood triangulation often creates internal parts — such as a caretaker part, a fearful child part, or a critical part — that continue to enact old relational roles.

Somatic therapy helps clients feel the bodily sensations linked to these parts, while IFS offers a compassionate framework to dialogue with them. This combination can untangle complex internal dynamics and promote healing integration.

The Role of Trauma-Informed Therapists and Safe Spaces

For survivors of triangulation, safety is paramount. Francine Shapiro, developer of EMDR therapy, underscores that trauma processing requires a stable, safe therapeutic environment. Somatic therapy practitioners prioritize creating this safety by attuning to clients’ nervous system cues and pacing interventions carefully.

Therapists trained in somatic and trauma-informed care can help survivors navigate triggers, regulate overwhelm, and build a new, embodied sense of safety that was missing in childhood.

Self-Directed Somatic Practices for Healing

While professional support is ideal, survivors can begin self-directed somatic work to build nervous system regulation and body awareness. Here are practical steps:

  • Mindful Body Scan: Spend 5-10 minutes daily noticing physical sensations without judgment.
  • Safe Place Visualization: Imagine a place where you feel secure and grounded; engage all senses.
  • Grounding Exercises: Press feet firmly into the floor, feel contact points of your body with your chair or bed.
  • Breath Awareness: Practice slow, even breathing (e.g., 4 seconds inhale, 6 seconds exhale) to engage the vagus nerve.
  • Movement: Gentle yoga, stretching, or shaking out tension can help discharge trapped stress energy.

As Gabor Maté emphasizes, healing trauma is a journey of reconnecting with the body’s wisdom and learning new ways to feel safe and whole.

Conclusion: Reclaiming Safety and Agency Through Somatic Healing

Childhood triangulation leaves deep imprints on the nervous system that affect emotional regulation, relationships, and self-worth. Somatic therapy offers survivors a powerful path to reclaim their bodies, regulate their nervous systems, and rewrite their internal narratives.

By cultivating body awareness, nervous system regulation, and compassionate self-inquiry, survivors can move beyond the stuck patterns left by triangulation and create authentic, grounded lives. As Dan Siegel writes, integration of mind, body, and relationships is the foundation of true healing.

If you’ve tried talk therapy and still feel stuck, consider exploring somatic approaches that meet trauma where it lives — in the body, not just the mind.

References:

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No.
  • Siegel, D. (2012). The Developing Mind.

faqs.a: Childhood triangulation occurs when a child is caught between conflicting caregivers, becoming an involuntary participant in their disputes or emotional dynamics.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy help with trauma from triangulation?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy works by helping survivors tune into bodily sensations and regulate the nervous system, addressing trauma stored in the body beyond just the mind.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy be combined with other trauma therapies?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic therapy complements approaches like Internal Family Systems and EMDR, enhancing trauma resolution by integrating body-based and cognitive processes.

faqs.q: What are some self-help somatic techniques for survivors?
faqs.a: Practices like mindful body scans, breath regulation, grounding exercises, and gentle movement can help survivors begin to regulate their nervous system safely.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and how somatic healing can restore safety and integration.

metaTitle: Somatic Therapy for Childhood Triangulation Survivors
metaDescription: Learn how somatic therapy helps survivors of childhood triangulation heal trauma and regulate their nervous system for lasting recovery.
author: The Oracle Lover
